People shopping in a local market in Southwark. Rebecca Towers from We Walworth writes about their successful collaboration with the UK's central government.

# Case study

## [Taking policy design to a global public audience](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/07/07/taking-policy-design-to-a-global-public-audience/)

![A photo of UK designers visiting the biennale](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2025/07/AK22-620x413.jpg)

UK Civil Service’s first Biennale pavilion has been a great success. Curators Andrew Knight and Elli-ana Morley report on the event.

## [Designing policy with complex evidence](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/05/08/designing-policy-with-complex-evidence/)

![Workshop participants discussing ‘evidence cards’](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2025/04/AT1-620x413.jpg)

Farming policy design experts from Defra get to grips with making sense of complexity.

## [Co-designing with pupils](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/04/10/co-designing-with-pupils/)

![A photo of a government official working with young people.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2025/04/AM6-620x413.jpg)

Education policy designers Alex Mathers and Chahat Gursingh are finding new ways to develop public services with young people.

## [Good services are verbs, but better services include nouns](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/03/13/good-services-are-verbs-but-better-services-include-nouns/)

![A photo of person experiencing a great service.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2025/02/TS3-620x413.jpg)

Defra policy lab experts Toby Squire and Rhiannon Long design public services around objects that are familiar to citizens.

## [Mapping the justice system](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/02/27/mapping-the-justice-system/)

![A person discussing public services.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2024/09/BM1-620x413.jpg)

Designer Antonio Perra from Ministry of Justice values mapping citizen experience and systems.

## [Latvian and British designers connect on public policy innovation](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/02/13/latvian-and-british-designers-connect-on-public-policy-innovation/)

![A photo of people taking part in a design workshop.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2025/01/UK4-620x413.png)

Uga Kalendris from Latvia's Innovation Laboratory reports on their visit to the UK

## [Sprint before you walk: fail faster](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2025/01/30/sprint-before-you-walk-fail-faster/)

![A photo of a person participating in a workshop.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2025/01/ME6-620x413.jpg)

Department for Education design leader Mark Edwards explains how his team are working in new ways to rapidly design public services.

## [Understanding people in places](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2024/03/15/understanding-people-in-places/)

![People shopping in a local market in Southwark.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2023/12/RT3-620x413.jpeg)

Rebecca Towers from We Walworth writes about their successful collaboration with the UK's central government.

## [Designing the world's most effective border](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2024/02/01/designing-the-worlds-most-effective-border/)

![A person working at a port.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2023/11/POS4-620x349.jpg)

Patrick O'Shea writes about how his team have gone about refreshing how the UK's border operates.

## [Embedding a user-centred design team into a policy area](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/2024/01/04/embedding-a-user-centred-design-team-into-a-policy-area/)

![A photo of public sector workers making a policy or service.](https://publicpolicydesign.blog.gov.uk/wp-content/uploads/sites/255/2023/11/CF2-620x413.jpg)

Claire Folan from the Department for Education writes about her experience of establishing a new policy lab in government.

--- SOURCE 4: 10 Examples of Design Thinking in Government ---
URL: https://www.design-thinking-association.org/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/10-examples-design-thinking-government

SUMMARY:
Using the methodology and mindset of Design Thinking in government projects can help to clarify for whom, what problems need to be solved and test and improve the resultant ideas quickly before huge resources are applied to rolling out the new or improved government services. Here are a number of resources and examples from taxation to healthcare, education to libraries and cities to strategy that can help you to get a better understanding of how to use Design Thinking in Government problem solving projects. 8. Also take a look at IBM's four case studies which demonstrate design thinking in government public service delivery improvements Using Design Thinking in government projects can help create new services as well as improve existing services within government.

FULL CONTENT:
[Skip to main content](#main-content)

## [10 Examples of Design Thinking in Government](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/10-examples-design-thinking-government)

[Read time: 3-4 minutes](/taxonomy/term/read-time-3-4-minutes)

Government's role is to provide essential services for its people. Using the methodology and mindset of Design Thinking in government projects can help to clarify for whom, what problems need to be solved and test and improve the resultant ideas quickly before huge resources are applied to rolling out the new or improved government services.

Governments mandate is clear and so is the target customer: it's citizens. All of them.

The problems of government are complex, i.e. affordable healthcare for all, simple collection of taxation, etc, etc. The task becomes all that much more challenging when you add the inevitable and mostly ugly politics that comes with government work into the mix.

Affordable healthcare for all. Three components.

* Provide healthcare: we know how to do that. BUT there is a lot of opportunity to improve it.
* Affordable: that adds many more challenges.
* For all: now we are deep into big hairy audacious problem solving territory.

Governments need to develop services in order to meet the needs of their citizens, so a lot of the problem solving of government involves the successful development and delivering of services. Governments are not set up like start-ups or even traditional product/service delivery organizations so the product/service creation function does not often exist in any real form within government. Using Design Thinking in government projects can help to set up projects for success.

[Here are a number of resources and examples from taxation to healthcare, education to libraries and cities to strategy that can help you to get a better understanding of how to use Design Thinking in Government problem solving projects.](https://www.linkedin.com/in/daver/)

1. [Dave Robertson](https://www.linkedin.com/in/daver/) presents two case studies with the [British Columbia Government (Canada)](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/design-thinking-public-engagement-two-case-studies) which discuss how to more effectively engage with constituents.
2. Jeffrey Ho, Executive Director of the DesignSingapore Council discusses how the [Singapore Government](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/how-design-thinking-reshaping-singapore) is using design thinking to improve Law and Order, Health centers and services and how Design Thinking is influencing policy making.
3. In another article [The Ministry of Manpower’s Work Pass Division (WPD) used design thinking as a tool to develop better ways to support foreigners](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/redesigning-employment-pass-application-singapore) who choose Singapore as a destination to live, work and set up businesses. The case reveals: Design thinking can potentially transform the perception and meaning of public service.
4. The US Tax Office has used the methodology in their [Tax Forms Simplification Project](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/us-tax-forms-simplification-project).
5. The US White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) has been working with agencies to identify and [share best practices](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/using-human-centered-design-make-government-work) to promote innovation and make government work better and cost less.
6. In 2004, the [Australian Taxation Office (ATO)](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/failure-launch-learning-about-design-hard-way) decided to design and build an enterprise content management system to streamline its publishing process. The first attempt revealed the technology of the day was inadequate for the design of this complicated project. However, 12 years later the ATO now has a world class website which is shaped by daily user feedback and longitudinal unmoderated usability benchmarking.
7. The Public Libraries in Aarhus (Denmark) and Chicago (USA), with funding from the Gates Foundation, created the [Design Thinking for Libraries toolkit](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/design-thinking-libraries) to introduce a way of working that will help you understand the needs of your patrons and engage your communities like never before.
8. Also take a look at [IBM's four case studies](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/applying-design-thinking-public-service-delivery) which demonstrate design thinking in government public service delivery improvements
   1. Engaging Employees at the Department of Health and Human Services (US Government: HHS)
   2. Aligning Diverse Stakeholder groups at the Food and Drug Administration (US Government: FDA)
   3. Improving solution quality at the Good Kitchen (Danish Government)
   4. Fostering Experimentation and Change at MasAgro (Mexican Government Collaboration with agriculture groups)
9. The US Government has also used Design Thinking quite extensively in projects, but also in work done by the Office of Personnel Managements "The Labs" facility to define Design Thinking skills, a [field guide for Design Thinking](/explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/lab-opm-field-guide) and a roll out of training across all government departments.
10. [Design Thinking in Elementary School](https://explore-design-thinking-topics/external-links/improving-school-experiences-and-helping-teachers) – David Lee discusses 3 projects his students did using design thinking.

In this short 5:44 video, Hailey Brewer of IDEO offers some guidance on how to start working on problems of government. It is worth a listen. Hailey suggests 5 ideas for starting points.

1. Beyond efficiency - START WITH PEOPLE
2. Beyond surveys - OBSERVE PEOPLE IN CONTEXT
3. Beyond the official process - PIGGYBACK ON EXISTING BEHAVIORS
4. Beyond the middle - LOOK AT EXTREMES
5. Beyond demographics - DESIGN FOR BEHAVIORS

Using Design Thinking in government projects can help create new services as well as improve existing services within government. It provides a new way to look at problems from the users point of view rather than from a political, business or technology point of view. Suppliers of services and services design to the government tend to be more focused on the former 3 than on the people they are designing for, so anytime you can focus the creation and design of services from the government on the actual needs of the people is a good day for us all!

 Click to view another 30+ articles about Design Thinking in Government

enriched_points.expanded_content: Case studies from the UK and Denmark illustrate effective design thinking applications, enhancing public services like education and healthcare through user-centered design approaches.
Key insights include the importance of involving stakeholders early, iterating designs based on feedback, and understanding complex user needs in public policy contexts.
Utilize rapid prototyping, foster collaboration among diverse teams, and embed user-centered practices into policy development to ensure impactful public service delivery.
